# Sketching sequences {#sec-intro-sketching}

*Sketching* is the idea of summarizing a (large) dataset into a (small) set of fingerprints, called a *sketch*.
A sketch is designed to be much cheaper to store and compare, while still providing good approximations for properties of interest.
In this chapter, we treat sequences as sets of /kmers (without accounting for their number of occurrences or positions) and we focus on sketches approximating the *similarity* of two sequences.

## Why do we want to sketch sequences?

The main reason to do sketching in the first place is, of course, the colossal amount of sequencing data we have to deal with.
What is especially limiting is not just the length the sequences themselves but mainly how many of them we have to compare, for instance when comparing an unknown sample against millions of genomes to find the closest matches.
In other words, sketching is generally used as an efficient *filtering* step prior to more expensive operations on a restricted set of sequences [@survey-sketching-rowe].
Therefore, we really focus on two goals when designing sketching methods: having *lightweight* sketches (because of how many of them we have to store), and supporting *fast* estimation of similarity (because of how many comparisons we have to make).
The memory footprint of a sketch is always a tradeoff with the *accuracy* of the estimate it can produce.

## Approximating similarity

The similarity of two genomic sequences is generally defined by their *average nucleotide identity* (ANI), which measures the expected probability that a nucleotide position in a shared genomic region is identical between two genomes [@ani].
However, computing ANI exactly is quite expensive so we typically rely on the similarity of the corresponding /kmer sets instead.
To measure the similarity of two sets $A$ and $B$, we use the *Jaccard index* (sometimes called Tanimoto coefficient) defined as
$$
J(A, B) = \frac{|A \cap B|}{|A \cup B|}
$$

Nevertheless, we still want to avoid having to intersect large /kmer sets.
Thus, instead of working with the original sets, we sample them into smaller sketches $S(A)$ and $S(B)$ for faster intersection.
@fig-jaccard-mash illustrate this idea for "bottom MinHash" sketching that we present in the next section.

:::{#fig-jaccard-mash}
![](https://media.springernature.com/full/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs13059-016-0997-x/MediaObjects/13059_2016_997_Fig1_HTML.gif?as=webp)

Overview of Jaccard similarity and how bottom MinHash can approximate it, from @mash.
Dots filled in blue represent the sketch of $A$, while those filled in red represent the sketch of $B$ and crosses indicate the sketch of $A \cup B$.
:::

## MinHash and its variations

Now that we know we want to sketch /kmer sets to quickly estimate their Jaccard similarity, the main question becomes: how do we design sketching methods that are as accurate as possible with a tight memory budget?

### MinHash

MinHash [@minhash] is one of the simplest solutions to this problem.
Given a regular hash function $h$, it computes the sketch of a set $A$ as
$$
h(A) = \min_{x \in A} h(x)
$$
The nice property of this definition is that the probability for the minimum hashes of two sets to be equal exactly matches their Jaccard index, in other words it is an *unbiased estimator*:
$$
\mathbb{P}(h(A) = h(B)) = J(A, B)
$$

### Bottom MinHash

### FracMinHash

## Bucketed sketches and smaller fingerprints

### Bucketed MinHash

### Low bits and HyperLogLog

## Other types of sketches

### SimHash and filters for set membership

### DotHash for intersection

### Sequence reduction for mapping and assembly
